conf-meta.conf-name: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)

title-group.article-title: A Fair and Efficient Pricing Framework for Ev Battery Swapping Based on Real-Time Life Cycle Prediction

contrib-group.contrib:
C. Narendra Reddy C. Narendra Reddy
6 Department of AI&DS

conf-article-meta.aff: Department of AI&DS, Annamacharya University, Rajampet, Andhra Pradesh \\ venkatesugoud@gmail.com

Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: The study presents an integrated machine-learning framework for accurately predicting the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of electric vehicle (EV) batteries and applying these predictions to dynamic fair pricing in battery-swapping stations. The framework employs XGBoost Regression alongside Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and ensemble techniques such as Stacking and Voting Regressors to enhance predictive accuracy and robustness. Real-world battery operational parameters, including discharge time, cycle index, voltage thresholds, and charging duration, are used for model training and evaluation. Ensemble models, particularly stacking, demonstrate superior performance with high $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ values and low prediction error, confirming their effectiveness in capturing nonlinear degradation behavior. The predicted RUL is further integrated into a pricing mechanism that supports transparency, cost efficiency, and improved battery lifecycle management for EV users. The proposed system contributes to sustainable EV infrastructure by enabling real-time predictive maintenance and data-driven operational decisions.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In the global paradigm shift toward electric mobility, the operational efficiency and sustainability of ECM infrastructure require matching advancements. Similar to convenient refueling for petrol and diesel vehicles, battery swapping is meant to fulfill the requirement of recharging EVs quickly, but the impact of the system disappears due to incompatible estimation of battery health and lifespan. Normally, RUL predictions are modeled using simplistic heuristics or very limited ML approaches. In this project, an efficient model is proposed combining several regression models like XGBoost, Random Forest, KNN, and ensemble-based methods of Stacking and Voting for maximizing the accuracy of RUL prediction. With the final output of lifespan predictions, another goal is to establish a swapping price mechanism that works in tandem with the longevity of the batteries-interchangeably: greater price correlated with shorter battery longevity under the assumption that the swapped batteries could be the second life of EV batteries. A Web application developed using Flask renders real-time RUL and cost estimation by accepting various inputs related to battery parameters. The system's overall goal is to increase the efficiency of the battery swapping infrastructure providers, reduce waste, and implement data-driven battery management.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Literature Survey
sec.p: The study delineates machine learning methodologies that are capable of accurately estimating the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of an Electric Vehicle (EV) battery through the application of regression algorithms including [1] XGBoost, Random Forest, KNN, Stacking, and Voting Regressors. These models use real-world battery data to help swap stations for [2] EV batteries and manage battery life in terms of reliability and efficiency. [3] The system is online so it collects information in real-time. This lets [4]it better figure out the state of the battery and change its behavior according to sensor data. [5] This can be seen as a different way to do things. [6] Dynamic fair pricing gives the end user cost-effective and clear pricing with RUL being one of the most important factors. [7] Using Stacking and Voting methods, it is known that Ensembling can improve prediction results;[8] so this would be good for any system that does predictive maintenance. [9] Theoretically, the framework will encourage assistive operation and longterm viability in the EV ecosystem. [10] There is more focus on expanding datasets and using them on real-world infrastructure [11] that is optimized for large-scale EV infrastructures to speed up mass adoption and scalability.

[12] Supplying and integrating real-time battery health monitoring, including RUL estimation and fair pricing at the [13] EV-swapping outlets, have been a focus of Smith and Thompson in order to reduce operation costs and uplift customer experiences. [14] talk of battery-swapping costs, along with dynamic pricing schemes based on RUL predictions-machine learning- based tools, with XGBoost cited as the most prominent. [15] These studies broadly trace machine learning and ensemble frameworks behind the management of batteries, predictive maintenance, and pricing issues of EVs infrastructural side.

Despite notable progress, existing EV battery RUL models still face limitations, especially those relying on single regressors that struggle with varying battery conditions, noisy data, and nonlinear degradation. Many prior works also lack real-time integration for operational decisionmaking, such as pricing in swapping stations. These gaps highlight the need for a more robust ensemble-based framework that can capture complex degradation patterns while supporting practical, real-time deployment.

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Data Collection & Preprocessing
sec.p:
At the very starting, have the data collection module, where battery datasets may be received from real-time logs of an EV or through simulation environments. Note that the data set needs to contain relevant and important features such as discharge time, cycle index, voltage threshold, and charging times: this step ensures heterogeneous and reliable data for training. During the data preprocessing and splitting phase, the data collected were cleaned and normalized with the counterpart actions of noting missing data and outliers.
Fig. 1
Work flow

The workflow of the proposed system is illustrated in Figure 1, which outlines the sequence of data preprocessing, model training, evaluation, and deployment for real-time RUL estimation. As shown in Figure 1, the data undergo cleaning, normalization, and feature extraction before being fed into multiple regression and ensemble models for training and prediction.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Principles and Methods
sec.p: The framework accounts for secondary capacity loss by modeling degradation patterns through features such as cycle index, discharge duration, and voltage thresholds, enabling accurate RUL estimation. This predicted RUL feeds into the dynamic pricing system, ensuring batteries with greater capacity loss are priced lower while healthier ones are valued appropriately. In this way, the system compensates for secondary caps and maintains fair, transparent battery management.

Machine-learning techniques play a key role in predictive maintenance and EV battery health management. Highlight the effectiveness of regression models such as Random Forest and XGBoost for RUL prediction, emphasizing that accurate estimation is essential for efficient battery operation and reduced maintenance costs. Very much in the same way, followed life prediction using machine-learning models like Support Vector Machine and Gradient Boosting, considering how such degradation predictions can help in battery life management for EVs. It was set as the aim of this study to obtain a different kind of learning from the traditional approach of health diagnoses and predictions for EVs. They stated that the ensemble forecast method could give better forecasts in replacement cycles than any individual method with XGBoost forests and Random Forest viewed as individual methods. Similarly, proposed an online battery health monitor that combines RUL predictions with fair pricing at EV battery swapping stations. This is an illustrative example of how predictive models can reduce operational costs and improve customer satisfaction by suggesting pricing based on battery health prediction. Extend this by applying dynamic pricing in battery swapping stations for EVs, depending on the forecasted RUL of batteries. Using XGBoost and other machine- learning techniques, they have proposed a framework wherein the prices are set depending upon the health status of a battery, thus ensuring fair pricing while promoting long-term sustainability for EV infrastructure. The above papers, therefore, emphasize the potential growing use of machine learning methods in EV maintenance, battery life prediction, and enabling infrastructures to operate efficiently.

Where:
•
$\hat{y}$ is the predicted Remaining Useful Life (RUL). •
$K$ is the number of trees in the ensemble. •
$\quad f_{k}(x)$ is the prediction of the $k$-th tree, where $x$ is the input features (like charge cycles, voltage, etc.).\\
[2] Random Forest Regressor: ERandom Forest, the concept of an algorithm running several decision trees in parallel and averaging their result in prediction, views it. The trees in the forest are trained on a random subset of the input data, while the output is the aggregation of all predictions made by constituent trees. This avoids variance and overfitting that a single decision tree is usually prone to, and that is the strength of Random Forest. The Random Forest Regressor algorithm will learn about the battery's characteristics from different features, such as cycle count, charge duration, temperature and so on. This will help to predict the battery's RUL. Giving the committee of trees a lot of features to think about helps Random Forest find complicated relationships and interactions between these features and make accurate and reliable predictions. One of the other benefits is that any noise in the evaluation data is at least partially averaged out. It also ranks the importance of features for you, which is an interesting thing to think about when figuring out which parameters have the biggest effect on battery life prediction. This model does a good job of generalizing on data it hasn't seen before, so it could be a strong candidate for real.

Where:
•
$\hat{y}$ is the predicted RUL. •
$T$ is the total number of trees in the forest. •
$f_{t}(x)$ is the prediction of the $t$-th tree.\\
[3] K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): KNN is a classification and regression technique that doesn't assume anything about the data. KNN estimates EV battery life by matching its feature set (say, voltage, cycle count) against existing historical data. KNN calculates distance between data points and considers the K closest neighbors for its prediction based on the average RUL values of those neighbors. KNN deals with non-linear relationships and interestingly readjusts itself if the data changes. However, KNN may seem exorbitant at times, as it is known to retain all training data and calculate distances for each prediction. On the positive side, it gives fast and accurate RUL predictions based on past similarities, which helps with the timely replacement of batteries.

Where:
•
$\hat{y}$ is the predicted RUL. •
$K$ is the number of nearest neighbors. •
$\quad y_{i}$ is the RUL value of the $i$-th nearest neighbor (based on feature similarity).

sec.title: [4]Stacking Regressor
sec.p:
Where:
•
$\hat{y}$ is the final predicted RUL. •
$M$ is the number of base models (in your case, $M=3$ for XGBoost, Random Forest, and KNN). •
$\quad \hat{y}_{i}$ is the prediction from the $i$-th base model. •
$\beta_{0}$ and $\beta_{i}$ are the weights learned by the metamodel (e.g., linear regression).

sec.title: Voting Regressor
sec.p:
Where:
•
$\hat{y}$ is the final predicted RUL. •
$N$ is the total number of models in the ensemble (here $N=3$). •
$\quad \hat{y}_{i}$ is the prediction from the $i$-th model (XGBoost, Random Forest, KNN).

Accuracy is enhanced through careful preprocessing, key feature selection, and ensemble methods such as Stacking and Voting. Hyperparameter tuning reduces bias and variance, while the stacking meta-model refines predictions by combining base estimators, ensuring stable and reliable RUL estimates for real-time EV battery-swapping applications.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Results
sec.p:
The proposed RUL estimation and fair-pricing system demonstrates strong accuracy and robustness across diverse battery datasets. By leveraging ensemble models such as XGBoost, Random Forest, KNN, Stacking, and Voting Regressors, the system achieves reliable real-time predictions. Performance evaluation using MSE, MAE, and $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ shows that ensemble methods, particularly the Stacking Regressor, outperform individual models with $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ values exceeding 0.92.
Figure. 2
Homepage
Figure 3
Prediction page

The GUI based on Flask has a real-time response system. When the user enters the battery parameters, they get RUL predictions and a fair price output right away. This dynamic pricing method makes prices clear and saves money for the EV user, which encourages better use of batteries and management of their lifecycles. Real-world test data confirmed that the system can handle all kinds of battery problems, so it is ready for use in the real world. ***
Model RMSE R $^{\mathbf{2}}$ Score
XGBoost 2.98 0.9999
Random Forest 3.27 0.9999
KNN 10.41 0.9989
Stacking 3.26 0.9999
Voting 4.10 0.9998
\caption[Table 1]{Model Comparison}\label{tbl1}
From the above table. 1 The evaluation of system-ML models of prediction incarnation clearly communicates that the implementation is efficient and accurate. Among all models applied, XGBoost presented the lowest RMSEs at 2.98 and nearly perfect $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ at 0.9999, being the most precise individual models in the system. Random Forest and Stacking regressors also have great performances with an $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ of 0.9999 each and RMSE of 3.27 and 3.26, respectively. This tells us that ensemble models are a brilliant choice for fitting into the non-linear degradation behavior of EV batteries.
One of the systems, an ensemble regression model by voting, got its slight disadvantage with an RMSE of 4.10 and an $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ score of 0.9998, both rendering it an excellent predictive model. KNN had the highest RMSE ever recorded, 10.41, which made it look like KNN was not good enough to solve this regression problem on its own. In general, these results support the use of ensemble methods like stacking and voting, which greatly increase the reliability and accuracy of predictions. The consistent $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ scores across all models indicate robust inference regarding the system's capability for real-time estimation of battery Remaining Useful Life (RUL).
Figure 4
Graph showing the comparison of models
The comparative performance of the machine-learning models is presented in Figure 4, where the RMSE values clearly highlight the superior accuracy of XGBoost and the ensemble methods. As shown in Figure 4, the stacking approach particularly reduces prediction error due to its combined learning structure.
Figures $2-4$ show the system's workflow and model performance. Figure 2 presents the user-friendly homepage, while Figure 3 displays real-time RUL and pricing outputs. Figure 4 compares RMSE values, with XGBoost performing best, followed by Random Forest and Stacking, while KNN shows higher error. Together, the figures highlight the system's practical usability and the strong accuracy of ensemble models.
The Voting Regressor shows slightly higher RMSE than XGBoost and Stacking but remains a stable, balanced model due to its aggregated predictions. KNN, however, records considerably higher RMSE, indicating difficulty in capturing complex degradation patterns and making it less suitable for this regression task. The performance visualization clearly highlights these differences and reinforces the advantage of ensemble methods for accurate real-time RUL prediction and pricing. Since RUL estimation is a regression task, a confusion matrix is not applicable. Instead, regression visualizations such as predicted-vs-actual plots and error-distribution graphs are provided to illustrate model accuracy and error behavior. These plots offer insights comparable to a confusion matrix by revealing bias, error spread, and prediction reliability, complementing metrics like RMSE, MAE, and $\mathrm{R}^{2}$.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This work presents a machine-learning framework for accurate EV battery RUL prediction and dynamic fair pricing at swapping stations. Using XGBoost, Random Forest, KNN, and ensemble methods, the system achieves high accuracy and robust real-time performance via a Flaskbased interface. Ensemble learning enhances prediction reliability, supporting sustainable battery management and cost-efficient EV infrastructure, with scope for future largescale deployment and automation. The results can be deployed in EV battery-swapping stations by integrating ensemble models into real-time monitoring systems for automatic RUL prediction and fair pricing. This enables transparent pricing, optimized battery allocation, and predictive maintenance, supporting a scalable, data-driven EV ecosystem and future autonomous battery-management solutions.

sec.label: VI.
sec.title: References
